title: Burns Heir

jokes.text: D'oh! [Screams]
jokes.setup: Opening credits sequence with Homer presumably getting hurt
jokes.punchline: Homer's signature exclamation of pain/frustration
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer catchphrase - perfect character integration and highly quotable, though routine by this point in series

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Lousy job. Nothing exciting ever happens.
jokes.setup: Homer complains about his boring job
jokes.punchline: Immediately followed by alarm and winning announcement
jokes.explanation: Classic ironic timing - setup is immediately contradicted by events

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: My job is so boring, I fall asleep at my desk every day.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I wish something exciting would happen for once.

dialogue.speaker: News Anchor
dialogue.line: We interrupt this program to announce that Homer Simpson has won the lottery!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo!

jokes.text: The job of industrial chimney sweep for a day! Whoo-hoo!
jokes.setup: Homer wins a 'prize' that's actually terrible manual labor
jokes.punchline: Homer's genuine enthusiasm for what's obviously a punishment job
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer character moment - his clueless optimism about obviously bad situations

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo! I won! I won! What's my prize?

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: You've won an all-expense paid trip to the Springfield Nuclear Power Plant to personally clean the cooling towers!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh! Wait... the power plant? Woo-hoo! That's where I work! I love that place!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: This is the best day of my life! I get to spend the whole day cleaning radioactive sludge!

jokes.text: Hey, this isn't such a great prize. Oh, well, this may be a dirty job... but the big guys at the top work even harder.
jokes.setup: Homer realizes the job sucks but tries to rationalize it
jokes.punchline: Cut to Burns getting a luxurious bath from Smithers
jokes.explanation: Excellent ironic juxtaposition - Homer's naive belief contrasted with Burns' extreme luxury

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, sure the job stinks, but I'm doing it for my family. Plus, Mr. Burns depends on me. I'm an important part of the nuclear power plant.

jokes.text: Careful, Smithers! That sponge has corners, you know. I'll go find a spherical one.
jokes.setup: Burns is getting bathed by Smithers
jokes.punchline: Burns' ridiculous complaint about sponge geometry and Smithers' obedient response
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist humor - Burns' impossible demands perfectly capture his character's extreme privilege and Smithers' subservience

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, this sponge is a rhombus. I specifically requested a parallelogram.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Right away, sir. I'll fetch the parallelogram sponge.

jokes.text: Help, Smithers. I'm sinking. Life... flashing... before-- [Gurgling] eyes.
jokes.setup: Burns dramatically claims to be drowning
jokes.punchline: He's clearly in a shallow bath, making his panic absurd
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy showing Burns' frailty and tendency toward melodrama

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Help! I'm drowning!

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Sir, you're in a bath.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: A bath can be a death trap, Smithers!

jokes.text: You're fired! Dance! [Laughing]
jokes.setup: Burns appears to be having a nightmare or fantasy
jokes.punchline: Burns gleefully fires someone and forces them to dance
jokes.explanation: Dark humor revealing Burns' sadistic fantasies about employee abuse

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: You're fired! Now dance!

jokes.text: It was I, you fools! The man you trusted isn't Wavy Gravy at all.
jokes.setup: Burns is apparently disguised as hippie activist Wavy Gravy
jokes.punchline: Burns reveals himself as the villain in an environmental scenario
jokes.explanation: Absurd image of Burns disguised as famous peaceful hippie, perfect character contradiction

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about the environment. We need to protect our forests and waterways.

dialogue.speaker: Environmental Activist
dialogue.line: Finally! A man of reason! Will you help us stop the toxic dumping?

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Of course! In fact, I'm shutting down my nuclear plant immediately.

dialogue.speaker: Environmental Activist
dialogue.line: Excellent! You're a true hero!

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Yes, I'm shutting it down... and replacing it with a toxic waste facility three times larger. Smithers, release the hounds!

jokes.text: And all this time, I've been smoking harmless tobacco.
jokes.setup: Burns reveals his deception in the environmental fantasy
jokes.punchline: The punchline that tobacco is 'harmless' - darkly ironic
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about tobacco industry - Burns would consider tobacco harmless compared to nuclear waste

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about our little environmental chat, and I realized I may have been misleading you.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: You see, tobacco is completely harmless. I've been smoking it for decades, and I'm in perfect health!

jokes.text: Oh, my God! Mr. Burns is dead! [Sobbing] Why do the good always die so young?
jokes.setup: Smithers finds Burns apparently dead
jokes.punchline: Calling Burns 'good' and 'young' when he's clearly evil and ancient
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment for Smithers - his delusional devotion to obviously evil Burns

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns! Oh no, you're not... you can't be...

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: What a good man. What a young man.

jokes.text: Due to my hectic schedule and lethargic sperm...
jokes.setup: Burns explains why he has no heir
jokes.punchline: Bluntly clinical description of his reproductive issues
jokes.explanation: Surprisingly frank discussion of Burns' age-related fertility issues - good shock humor

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: I have no heir because I'm unable to reproduce. You see, my testicles are so withered and diminished that they're practically vestigial. And my penis, well, it hasn't worked since the Eisenhower administration.

jokes.text: When I pass on, you shall be buried alive with me. Oh. Goody.
jokes.setup: Burns reveals Smithers' 'reward' for loyalty
jokes.punchline: Smithers' unenthusiastic response to being buried alive
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor - Burns' casual mention of ancient burial practice met with Smithers' deadpan resignation

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, as a reward for your unwavering loyalty, I'm going to bury you alive.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Oh, thank you sir. That's very kind of you.

jokes.text: My vast, vast, vast fortune. Vast.
jokes.setup: Burns describes his wealth to potential heirs
jokes.punchline: Repetitive emphasis on 'vast' showing his ego and need for attention
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Burns' ego and showmanship

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: My vast fortune is unimaginable.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: My vast estate spans three counties.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: My vast collection of priceless art is worth more than your entire town.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: My vast influence reaches into every corner of this state.

jokes.text: Let's all go to the lobby, let's all go to the lobby, let's all go to the lobby, get ourselves some snacks
jokes.setup: Burns is forced to sing movie theater intermission song
jokes.punchline: Powerful billionaire reduced to performing cheesy movie theater jingle
jokes.explanation: Absurd humiliation of Burns, good visual of him being forced to perform

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: ♪ Let's all go to the lobby, let's all go to the lobby, let's all go to the lobby... ♪

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: A powerful billionaire, reduced to performing a cheesy movie theater jingle.

jokes.text: I have nothing to offer you but my love. I specifically said 'no geeks.' But my mom says I'm cool.
jokes.setup: Nerdy kid tries to win Burns over with love
jokes.punchline: Burns' harsh rejection followed by kid's pathetic defense
jokes.explanation: Good cringe humor with Martin's earnest attempt met by Burns' cruelty

dialogue.speaker: Martin
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns, sir, I've always admired you. Your cruel indifference to human suffering, your neck wattles... I love you.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Good Lord, man! Get away from me! You're like a baby seal at a club convention!

dialogue.speaker: Martin
dialogue.line: But sir, I can change! I can be less likable!

jokes.text: Give me your fortune or I'll pound your withered old face in! Oh, I like his energy. Put him on the callback list.
jokes.setup: Bully threatens Burns with violence
jokes.punchline: Burns appreciates the aggression and considers him seriously
jokes.explanation: Dark humor showing Burns values aggression and cruelty over kindness

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: Hey old man, I'm gonna pound you!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Excellent. That sort of aggressive initiative is exactly what I look for in my employees. You're hired.

dialogue.speaker: Nelson
dialogue.line: What? No, I was gonna—

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, draw up the paperwork. This young man has spunk.

jokes.text: I don't know what 'phallocentric' means, but no girls!
jokes.setup: Feminist makes intellectual argument about gender equality
jokes.punchline: Burns admits ignorance but maintains sexist position anyway
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Burns' willful ignorance and old-fashioned prejudices

dialogue.speaker: Feminist Character
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns, women deserve equal pay for equal work. It's a matter of basic fairness and economic justice.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Equal pay? Bah! I don't understand a word of what you just said, but I'm sure it's all nonsense. Women belong in the home, where they can properly serve their husbands and raise children. That's the natural order of things.

jokes.text: So much for Plan B.
jokes.setup: Lisa's feminist argument is immediately shot down
jokes.punchline: Her matter-of-fact acceptance that her intellectual approach failed
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa moment - intelligent but resigned to adult world's limitations

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Well, actually, if we examine the patriarchal structures that...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Shut up, Lisa.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: ...I suppose that's a fair point.

jokes.text: I started a fire this morning that I really should keep an eye on.
jokes.setup: Bart doesn't want to audition
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of starting and abandoning a fire
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart character moment - casual destructiveness treated as normal responsibility

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I'm not auditioning for the school play.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why not, boy?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Because I'll probably just start it and then abandon it, like that fire I started.

jokes.text: Hello, Marge. I'm Lee Majors. Will you come away with me? Uh... sure. [Bionic Jumping Sound]
jokes.setup: Marge fantasizes about Bart's future success
jokes.punchline: Fantasy shifts to Six Million Dollar Man actor Lee Majors taking her away
jokes.explanation: Unexpected shift from maternal pride to personal romantic fantasy - good character depth

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh, look at my Bart, all grown up and successful...

dialogue.speaker: Lee Majors
dialogue.line: Hello, Marge. I'm Lee Majors.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh my!

dialogue.speaker: Lee Majors
dialogue.line: Come away with me.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: But what about Bart?

dialogue.speaker: Lee Majors
dialogue.line: We have the technology. We can rebuild him.

jokes.text: Hello, Mr.-- Kurns. I bad want... money now. Me sick.
jokes.setup: Homer wrote Bart a script to win over Burns
jokes.punchline: Script is written in broken English like Homer doesn't understand grammar
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer incompetence - writes like he doesn't speak English as first language

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, this script is terrible. It's all broken English.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What you mean 'terrible'? I write perfect. Mr. Burns, he gonna love it.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Homer, you can't even speak English right.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Me speak English good. You shut up.

jokes.text: Ooh, he card reads good.
jokes.setup: Bart badly reads Homer's script
jokes.punchline: Homer's comment is itself grammatically incorrect
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - praising reading ability while demonstrating his own illiteracy

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, you're script has a lot of mistakes in it.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No it don't.

jokes.text: The boot kicked Bart! It kicked him right in the butt!
jokes.setup: Bart gets kicked by some kind of mechanical boot
jokes.punchline: Homer's excited commentary on his son getting hurt
jokes.explanation: Basic slapstick with Homer's inappropriate enthusiasm for violence against Bart

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo! Mechanical boot!

jokes.text: Kids, you tried your best, and you failed miserably. The lesson is, never try.
jokes.setup: Homer gives his children a life lesson after their failure
jokes.punchline: Completely wrong moral lesson delivered with paternal authority
jokes.explanation: Legendary quote - subverts expected parental wisdom with perfectly Homer-esque wrong lesson. Became cultural touchstone.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Kids, I want to talk to you about failure. You see, when I was your age, I failed at everything I tried.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But I didn't let that stop me. I just kept failing until nobody expected anything from me anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And that's when I truly became free. So remember: if at first you don't succeed, give up. There's no point in trying.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Now if you'll excuse me, all this parenting has made me thirsty.

jokes.text: Eggs have gotten quite a bad rap lately, you know, Smithers.
jokes.setup: Burns considers leaving money to Egg Advisory Council
jokes.punchline: Burns' concern for eggs' public image reputation
jokes.explanation: Absurd concern for food product's reputation - good random Burns moment

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I'm thinking of leaving my fortune to the Egg Advisory Council.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Sir, that's very generous.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Yes, well, eggs have done so much for me. But I'm concerned about their public image. People think eggs are high in cholesterol.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Perhaps a charitable campaign, sir?

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Exactly! We'll tell people eggs are nutritious, delicious, and... they come from chickens, for God's sake!

jokes.text: Oh, look. A bird has become petrified and lost its sense of direction. I think it's a rock, sir. We'll see what the lab has to say about that.
jokes.setup: Burns sees Bart throwing rocks and breaking windows
jokes.punchline: Burns thinks a rock is a petrified bird, Smithers corrects him, Burns insists on lab analysis
jokes.explanation: Good Burns characterization - completely disconnected from reality but stubbornly confident

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: What ho! A petrified bird!

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Actually sir, that's just a rock.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Nonsense, Smithers. I know a petrified bird when I see one. Have it sent to the lab for analysis.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Yes, sir.

jokes.text: A creature of pure malevolence. He's the perfect one to suckle at my proverbial teat.
jokes.setup: Burns watches Bart breaking windows
jokes.punchline: Burns' disturbing maternal metaphor for evil mentorship
jokes.explanation: Disturbing and funny metaphor mixing maternal nurturing with evil corruption

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, look at that boy breaking windows. Such raw talent for destruction. I must take him under my wing.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Sir, perhaps that's not advisable—

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Nonsense! I shall mold him, nurture him, be a mother to his wickedness. We'll be like a hen and her evil chick, huddled together in the dark coop of villainy.

jokes.text: Today, sir? Why, it's Christmas Day. I was talking to him.
jokes.setup: Burns asks 'what day is this' like Scrooge in Christmas Carol
jokes.punchline: Random kid gives Christmas Carol response, Burns clarifies he wants Bart to answer
jokes.explanation: Reference to Christmas Carol with Burns channeling Scrooge, mildly clever literary reference

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: What day is this?

dialogue.speaker: Random Kid
dialogue.line: Why, it's Christmas Day!

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: No, no, I meant you, Simpson boy. What day is this?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Uh, Monday?

jokes.text: We want to get the old people smell out before we move in. Dad, Mr. Burns hasn't passed away yet.
jokes.setup: Homer tells Bart to air out Burns' mansion
jokes.punchline: Homer's premature planning for Burns' death, Bart's reality check
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about Homer's eagerness for Burns to die, Bart being more grounded

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Alright boy, I want you to go air out Mr. Burns' mansion.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Why? Is he dead?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Not yet. But I like to be prepared.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, he's only like fifty.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Fifty?! That's ancient! He could go any minute now!

jokes.text: Are you thinking what I'm thinking? Yeah. Let's push him down the steps. No!
jokes.setup: Homer and Marge look at frail old Burns on steps
jokes.punchline: Both think of violence but Marge meant something else entirely
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - audience expects Marge to be voice of reason but she's thinking violence too

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Look at the old guy. One push and he'd tumble right down those stairs.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer! I was thinking we could gently suggest he install a handrail.

jokes.text: How come Bart gets to do that and I can't spend one night lurking in the bushes outside Chef Boyardee's house?
jokes.setup: Lisa complains about unfair treatment regarding Bart's situation
jokes.punchline: Her desire to stalk the Chef Boyardee mascot
jokes.explanation: Unexpected Lisa moment - reveals surprisingly creepy obsession with food mascot

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Why does Bart get all the attention? Nobody cares what I think.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm going to stalk the Chef Boyardee mascot. At least he'll listen to me.

jokes.text: Bart, I know you children see me as some sort of 'booger man'... but, uh, I'm really not such a bad... dude.
jokes.setup: Burns tries to connect with Bart using kid-friendly language
jokes.punchline: Ancient evil billionaire saying 'booger man' and 'dude' awkwardly
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns character moment - trying and failing to connect with modern youth slang

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Greetings, young man. I hear you youngsters enjoy the 'booger man' and saying 'dude' to one another.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Uh, Mr. Burns, nobody says that.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Nonsense! I am hip to the times. Now let us engage in some radical bonding, dude. Perhaps we can discuss the booger man?

jokes.text: Whoa! [Muffled Grunting] Oh, sorry. Wrong button.
jokes.setup: Burns tries to ring for maid
jokes.punchline: Accidentally triggers trapdoor or similar mechanism on Bart
jokes.explanation: Basic slapstick showing Burns' mansion has dangerous mechanisms

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I'll just ring for the maid.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: What the—?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Uh, Mr. Burns? You just activated the trapdoor. You might want to step back.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Trapdoor? What trapdoor? There's no trapdoor in my—

jokes.text: It's made from hooves, you know.
jokes.setup: Burns offers Bart gelatin as a treat
jokes.punchline: Burns points out gelatin's disgusting origin matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Burns' disconnect from what kids find appetizing - truthful but off-putting

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Here, boy. Have some gelatin.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Uh, thanks, Mr. Burns.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: You know, it's made from the boiled bones and hooves of dead animals.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Ew!

jokes.text: Hey, it's Krusty the Pizza Man! All right. Where's my 400 bucks?
jokes.setup: Bart wanted pizza delivered by Krusty the Clown
jokes.punchline: Krusty's immediate focus on payment over entertainment
jokes.explanation: Good Krusty characterization - always focused on money over entertainment value

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Hey kid, you want pizza delivered by Krusty the Clown?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah!

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: That'll be fifty bucks. Cash only.

jokes.text: How can you be here when your show's on live? Ah, I just threw on an old rerun. No one will know the difference.
jokes.setup: Bart questions the logic of Krusty being there during his show
jokes.punchline: Krusty's casual disregard for his audience and professional duties
jokes.explanation: Good meta humor about TV production and Krusty's cynical approach to his show

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wait, Krusty, shouldn't you be doing your show right now?

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Ah, the show? It's on a tape loop from 1987. The audience doesn't know the difference, and frankly, neither do I.

jokes.text: The Falkland Islands have just been invaded. I repeat, the Falklands have just been invaded!
jokes.setup: Instead of Krusty's show, old news footage plays
jokes.punchline: Serious 1982 Falklands War news presented as children's entertainment
jokes.explanation: Absurd juxtaposition of serious historical news with children's programming

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: Good evening. We interrupt Krusty the Clown for this special report. British forces have invaded the Falkland Islands.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: Argentine soldiers are scrambling to defend their position. Here's a fun fact for the kiddies: a single Exocet missile can level an entire city block!

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: Back to you, Krusty!

jokes.text: I got the idea from that movie, Sliver. What a delightful romp.
jokes.setup: Burns explains his surveillance system inspiration
jokes.punchline: Burns describes creepy voyeurism thriller as 'delightful romp'
jokes.explanation: Burns' completely wrong interpretation of disturbing thriller as light entertainment

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: I got the idea for my surveillance system from a delightful romp I saw at the cinema. A voyeurism thriller about a man who watches women through their windows.

jokes.text: Are you talking to me? There's no one else here. You must be talking to me.
jokes.setup: Wiggum is alone doing mirror routine
jokes.punchline: Famous Taxi Driver mirror scene performed by incompetent police chief
jokes.explanation: Movie reference humor with Wiggum doing famous De Niro scene - mildly amusing

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: You talkin' to me? You talkin' to me? Well, I'm the only one here.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Who the hell else you think you're talkin' to? Oh, yeah? You best watch it, pal.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: I'm gonna bust you up. Ah, forget about it.

jokes.text: Oh, that explains his mysterious trip to Holland.
jokes.setup: Burns watches someone through surveillance
jokes.punchline: Burns draws conclusions about travel based on observed behavior
jokes.explanation: Weak voyeurism joke - implies something happened but doesn't specify what

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I've been watching Homer through the surveillance cameras. He leaves work at 5 PM, arrives home at 5:15. Then at 6 PM he's back at work. The man's commute is only 15 minutes, yet he spends an hour away. I can only conclude... he's traveling to Europe.

jokes.text: Mr. Burns's house has everything-- a hedge maze, a moat, bleached hardwood floors and a bottomless pit.
jokes.setup: Bart describes luxurious amenities at Burns' mansion
jokes.punchline: List escalates from luxury items to deadly trap
jokes.explanation: Good escalation joke - moves from realistic luxury to absurd danger

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns has a solid gold toilet, a moat filled with sharks, a dungeon with torture devices, and a room where he breeds man-eating tigers.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, a solid gold toilet? That's amazing!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, and the sharks have frickin' laser beams attached to their heads.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Cool! What else?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: And the tiger room has a trapdoor that opens up to a pit of alligators wearing tiny hats.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Tiny hats?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, and at the bottom of the pit is a self-destruct button that destroys the entire mansion.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why would Mr. Burns have that?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I dunno. I guess in case he ever gets tired of living.

jokes.text: It couldn't possibly be bottomless. For all intents and purposes.
jokes.setup: Lisa logically challenges Bart's 'bottomless pit' claim
jokes.punchline: Bart's casual dismissal of physics with adult phrase
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast - Lisa's logic vs Bart's casual acceptance of impossible things

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, that's physically impossible. You can't have a bottomless pit.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Whatever, man. I'm not interested in your nerd facts.

jokes.text: Mr. Burns throws peas at Smithers. Ow! That was a big one!
jokes.setup: Bart justifies throwing peas by citing Burns' behavior
jokes.punchline: Smithers gets hit by pea while being discussed
jokes.explanation: Simple physical comedy with callback to established Burns/Smithers dynamic

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Well, Mr. Burns throws money around like it's nothing, so I figured I could throw a few peas.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: But sir, that's hardly the same thing—

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Then how do you explain this?

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Ow!

jokes.text: Oh, yuck. Meat loaf. My most hated of all loafs.
jokes.setup: Bart rejects dinner
jokes.punchline: Categorizes meat loaf among other types of loafs
jokes.explanation: Mild wordplay treating 'loaf' as a category of foods rather than cooking method

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Bart, dinner's ready. I made your favorite - meatloaf.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Meatloaf? No way, man. That's not food, that's a category. You got your meatloaf, your loaf of bread, your loaf pan... I'm not eating something that's just a loaf of meat.

jokes.text: That was the end piece! That's it! Being abusive to your family is one thing... but I will not stand idly by and watch you feed a hungry dog!
jokes.setup: Bart gives food to Santa's Little Helper
jokes.punchline: Marge prioritizes food waste over family abuse
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Marge's skewed priorities - food waste worse than family dysfunction

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Bart! Don't waste food! There are starving people in... in...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: ...between here and the refrigerator?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: No! Around the world!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: But Mom, I'm just feeding Santa's Little Helper.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well... that's different. Wasting food is wrong!

jokes.text: Mr. Burns nurtures my destructive side. I'm suffocating here.
jokes.setup: Bart explains why he wants to stay with Burns
jokes.punchline: Bart frames enabling his bad behavior as emotional support
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about child preferring toxic enabler over loving but restrictive family

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns said I could stay with him as long as I need. He really understands me.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But Bart, he's a monster who grinds up puppies for fun!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, but he's MY monster, Dad. He believes in me. He says I can be anything I want to be.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Sweetie, he's just using you.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: No way, man. He's my emotional support billionaire.

jokes.text: Oh, go eat some flowers! [Screams] My secret shame.
jokes.setup: Bart gives random insult to Homer
jokes.punchline: Homer's mortified reaction reveals he actually does eat flowers
jokes.explanation: Excellent unexpected revelation - random insult accidentally hits on Homer's actual embarrassing secret

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hey Dad, you eat flowers!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh! Why would you... I mean, who told you that?!

jokes.text: Or what? You'll release the dogs or the bees... or dogs with bees in their mouth, and when they bark, they shoot bees at you?
jokes.setup: Homer challenges Burns' threat
jokes.punchline: Increasingly ridiculous escalation of Burns' potential weapons
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation from realistic threats to absurd bee-shooting dogs. Became highly quotable meme.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Well what are you gonna do, Burns? You gonna use your army of robots?

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Hmm, yes, my robot army. Though I also have my tank battalion, my helicopter squadron, and my nuclear warhead collection.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, that's nothing! I've got... uh... a really sharp rock!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Foolish Simpson. I also possess a death ray, a giant laser, and a weather control machine.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I have... two sharp rocks!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: And I have a machine that turns people inside out, a shrink ray, and the ability to control minds through television signals.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's... that's not fair!

jokes.text: Oh, for gosh-- Can't you people solve these problems yourselves? I mean, we can't be 'policing' the whole city.
jokes.setup: Marge calls police about kidnapped child
jokes.punchline: Police chief complains about having to do police work
jokes.explanation: Perfect Wiggum characterization - completely missing the point of police work

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Chief Wiggum, my child has been kidnapped!

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Kidnapped? Now Marge, I'm going to have to do some actual police work, and frankly, I'm not prepared for that.

jokes.text: Why don't I drink out of a toilet bowl?
jokes.setup: Hutz is offered partially consumed Orange Julius
jokes.punchline: Hutz's disgusted comparison to toilet water
jokes.explanation: Basic gross-out humor showing Hutz's standards despite being a hack lawyer

dialogue.speaker: Lionel Hutz
dialogue.line: Well, I've seen toilet water with more appeal than this.

jokes.text: I've argued in front of every judge in the state, often as a lawyer.
jokes.setup: Hutz brags about his court experience
jokes.punchline: 'Often as a lawyer' implies he's also appeared as defendant
jokes.explanation: Excellent wordplay revealing Hutz's criminal past through careful phrasing

dialogue.speaker: Lionel Hutz
dialogue.line: I've appeared in court many times as a lawyer.

dialogue.speaker: Lionel Hutz
dialogue.line: Often as a lawyer, I mean. Not as a defendant. Well, mostly not as a defendant.

jokes.text: Uh, Judge, these won't be ready until Thursday. You know, we should really stop hiring him.
jokes.setup: Judge appears to rule based on DNA test
jokes.punchline: Test results aren't even ready yet, revealing court's incompetence
jokes.explanation: Good institutional incompetence joke showing Springfield's broken legal system

dialogue.speaker: Judge Snyder
dialogue.line: Based on these DNA test results, I find the defendant... guilty!

dialogue.speaker: Court Clerk
dialogue.line: Your Honor, the DNA results aren't back yet.

dialogue.speaker: Judge Snyder
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, in that case... not guilty!

jokes.text: My parents use that old love excuse to screw me out of toys.
jokes.setup: Milhouse envies Bart's material wealth
jokes.punchline: Frames parental love as manipulation tactic to avoid buying things
jokes.explanation: Great child's perspective - seeing emotional support as cheap substitute for material goods

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Bart, you're so lucky. Your parents buy you everything.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Not everything.

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Well, they love you. That's something I don't have.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: My parents love me? That's just their way of not buying me stuff.

jokes.text: Five, 23, skidoo. Hut, hut!
jokes.setup: Burns tries to be Bart's playground friend
jokes.punchline: Uses outdated slang and wrong sports terminology
jokes.explanation: Burns' completely out-of-touch attempt to connect with youth through ancient slang

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Well hello there, my boy. Shall we engage in some radical skate-boarding? I have my roller skates right here.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Uh, Mr. Burns, those are ice skates. And nobody says 'radical' anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Nonsense! Now, how about a rousing game of baseball? I'll pitch, you hit it with the hockey stick.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: That's... not how any of this works.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Come now, don't be such a party pooper! As the kids say... word to your mother!

jokes.text: Oh, pish-posh. There's a Jaws Of Life in the trunk.
jokes.setup: Bart says he doesn't know how to drive
jokes.punchline: Burns casually mentions emergency rescue equipment for expected crashes
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about Burns' casual attitude toward child endangerment

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I don't know how to drive.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Well, I've had three crash helmets installed in the car, and the seats are pre-positioned for impact. The windshield is reinforced, and airbags are supplemented by a full ejector seat.

jokes.text: I'm the one who successfully deprogrammed Jane Fonda, you know. What about Peter Fonda? Oh, that was a heartbreaker.
jokes.setup: Deprogrammer brags about celebrity successes
jokes.punchline: Confused different Fonda sibling with different ideologies
jokes.explanation: Cultural reference humor about confusing politically active Jane Fonda with apolitical Peter Fonda

dialogue.speaker: Deprogrammer
dialogue.line: I've deprogrammed all sorts of celebrities. Jane Fonda, Peter Fonda, all the Fondas really.

dialogue.speaker: Deprogrammer
dialogue.line: Jane was way too liberal, Peter was way too conservative. I straightened 'em both out.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But they're siblings with opposite politics, so you basically just swapped their beliefs?

dialogue.speaker: Deprogrammer
dialogue.line: Well, yes, but they're much happier now!

jokes.text: But I did get Paul McCartney out of Wings. You idiot! He was the most talented one!
jokes.setup: Deprogrammer brags about extracting McCartney from Wings
jokes.punchline: Homer correctly points out McCartney WAS Wings' main talent
jokes.explanation: Music knowledge joke with Homer being surprisingly right about Beatles/Wings

dialogue.speaker: Deprogrammer
dialogue.line: I extracted Paul McCartney from the cult Wings.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But he WAS Wings!

jokes.text: Homer, that's Hans Moleman. Can I keep him anyway? Huh? Huh?
jokes.setup: Family thinks they got Bart back
jokes.punchline: It's actually elderly Hans Moleman, Homer wants to keep him as pet
jokes.explanation: Good absurd humor with Homer treating elderly man like a pet to be adopted

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Bart! Oh thank goodness you're home!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Son! We were so worried!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Wait... this isn't Bart.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It's Hans Moleman!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: We have to return him at once.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But Marge, can't we keep him? He's like the son I never had!

jokes.text: Homer mistakes Hans Moleman for Bart after deprogramming
jokes.setup: Family reunion after getting Bart back
jokes.punchline: Homer embracing completely wrong person
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag setup, though the payoff is fairly predictable once the mistake is made.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Bart! Oh, Bart, I'm so glad you're back!

dialogue.speaker: Hans Moleman
dialogue.line: Actually, I'm Hans Moleman.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I don't care who you are, you're my boy!

jokes.text: That was delightful. Did you see that? That mouse butchered that cat like a hog!
jokes.setup: Burns watches violent cartoon
jokes.punchline: Burns' enthusiastic description of cartoon violence with weird simile
jokes.explanation: Burns enjoying cartoon violence with oddly specific butchery comparison

dialogue.speaker: C. Montgomery Burns
dialogue.line: Ah yes, here come the fireworks. Oh, how I love the sound of automatic weapons fire. It's like a thousand angels weeping.

jokes.text: I'm afraid your family doesn't want you back.
jokes.setup: Bart wants to go home
jokes.punchline: Burns manipulates child with fake family rejection
jokes.explanation: Dark setup for Burns' deception, more plot advancement than comedy

dialogue.speaker: Bart Simpson
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns, I want to go home.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Your family doesn't want you, boy. I checked. They laughed when I mentioned your name.

dialogue.speaker: Bart Simpson
dialogue.line: That's not true!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Suit yourself. But you'll always have a home here at the plant. Unlike your parents, I actually care about you.

jokes.text: Is he supposed to have some kind of neurological impairment... like Rain Man or Awakenings?
jokes.setup: Actor struggles to understand Homer character
jokes.punchline: Compares Homer to mentally disabled movie characters
jokes.explanation: Meta commentary on Homer's intelligence through outside perspective

dialogue.speaker: Actor
dialogue.line: I'm having trouble understanding this Homer character. What's his motivation?

dialogue.speaker: Director/Writer
dialogue.line: Well, he's just a regular guy trying to get by.

dialogue.speaker: Actor
dialogue.line: So he's like Forrest Gump?

dialogue.speaker: Director/Writer
dialogue.line: Sure, if you want to think of it that way.

dialogue.speaker: Actor
dialogue.line: Except Forrest Gump actually accomplishes things.

jokes.text: And this dialogue has none of the wit and sparkle of Murphy Brown.
jokes.setup: Actor continues complaining about the writing
jokes.punchline: Unfavorably compares Simpsons to Murphy Brown
jokes.explanation: Self-deprecating humor comparing show to contemporary sitcom

dialogue.speaker: Actor
dialogue.line: Unfavorably compares Simpsons to Murphy Brown

jokes.text: Oh! That rib always breaks.
jokes.setup: Burns gets hurt during father-son bonding
jokes.punchline: Casual acceptance of specific recurring injury
jokes.explanation: Burns' extreme frailty treated as routine inconvenience

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Why, I'm dying!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh my God! Dad, are you okay?

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Yes, yes, I'm fine. Just my spine again.

jokes.text: Just trying to fill the void of random, meaningless destruction... that Bart's absence has left in our hearts.
jokes.setup: Lisa explains why she's being destructive
jokes.punchline: Articulate psychological analysis of missing Bart's chaos
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa moment - intellectualizing emotional response to family dynamics

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I know this seems destructive, but I'm actually experiencing a profound sense of loss. Without Bart's constant chaos and unpredictability, my carefully ordered world has become... hollow. I miss the dialectical tension between his anarchic energy and my rational structure. We need each other to define ourselves.

jokes.text: If you can tell me why I shouldn't fire you without using the letter 'E'... you can keep your job.
jokes.setup: Burns creates impossible task for employee
jokes.punchline: Arbitrary linguistic restriction for job security
jokes.explanation: Creative wordplay challenge showing Burns' arbitrary cruelty

dialogue.speaker: Montgomery Burns
dialogue.line: I have a task for you. You must complete it by tomorrow or you're fired.

dialogue.speaker: Employee
dialogue.line: What is it, Mr. Burns?

dialogue.speaker: Montgomery Burns
dialogue.line: You must write a sentence without using the letter E.

dialogue.speaker: Employee
dialogue.line: But sir, that's nearly impossible!

dialogue.speaker: Montgomery Burns
dialogue.line: I don't pay you to make excuses. Now scram!

jokes.text: The fabulous toys, a lifetime of wealth and luxury... the gift certificate from Blockbuster Video... the freedom to do whatever you want.
jokes.setup: Burns lists what Bart will lose if he fires Homer
jokes.punchline: Mix of grand promises with mundane Blockbuster gift certificate
jokes.explanation: Bathos humor mixing grand wealth with small gift certificate

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: You'll lose your father's salary, your house, your car, your television, your stereo, your dog, your cat, and your Blockbuster gift certificate.

jokes.text: Because one of them hung out with me for a week, trying to get my character down. Yeah, me too. That midget taught me a lot about his native Estonia.
jokes.setup: Family explains how they know the actors weren't really them
jokes.punchline: Marge's random comment about learning Estonian culture from dwarf actor
jokes.explanation: Random cultural detail about actor's background - mildly absurd

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: We knew they weren't really us because they were way too good-looking.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: And I learned all about Estonian culture from that dwarf actor.

jokes.text: Cowabunga, dudes.
jokes.setup: Hans Moleman is introduced as new family member
jokes.punchline: Elderly man using outdated youth slang
jokes.explanation: Mild humor from generational clash in slang usage

dialogue.speaker: Hans Moleman
dialogue.line: Hello, I am Hans. Hans Moleman. Many people know me.

dialogue.speaker: Hans Moleman
dialogue.line: Say, that's totally radical, dude! And this is what you call 'sick,' yes? Or perhaps 'phat'? I am down with the youth culture.

jokes.text: Give it a try. It's like kissing a peanut.
jokes.setup: Homer encourages family to accept Hans Moleman
jokes.punchline: Homer's oddly specific comparison for kissing elderly man
jokes.explanation: Weird tactile comparison that's somehow both specific and inexplicable

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Come on, family. Hans Moleman is a human being. We should treat him with respect. Sure, he's been hit by a car 203 times, but that doesn't make him a bad person.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: But Homer, he tried to kiss you.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, kissing an old man is like kissing a wrinkly balloon filled with old man juice.

tmdb_overview: Bart is picked to be Burns' heir and moves into the Burns mansion, prompting Homer and Marge to hire a deprogrammer to get him back.
insight: Adopts sitcom-style plot for 70 jokes in 22 minutes—strongest character-comedy density of Season 5.
